content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting for Robust Code Generation with Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Most prompting methods for code generation focus on providing correct and canonical code snippets or instructions, but models remain brittle to edge-case or adversarial inputs. There is limited exploration of exposing models to 'negative' or counterfactual code examples during prompting.",
  "motivation": "Robust and generalizable code generation is essential as LLMs are deployed in real-world software engineering settings, where subtle bugs and unconventional code patterns are common. Conventional prompts may cause models to overfit to frequent patterns, missing edge cases or producing brittle code. Leveraging counterfactuals can foster deeper model reasoning and error avoidance.",
  "method": "We propose 'counterfactual prompting', where prompts not only provide positive examples (e.g., correct code) but also negative or minimally-edited buggy code snippets followed by explanations or corrections. The model is explicitly prompted to distinguish between valid and invalid code patterns, and to generate code while avoiding the negative patterns. Prompt templates include interleaved <incorrect snippet, explanation, correct snippet> triples. We hypothesize this will improve both accuracy and robustness to malformed input specifications.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on standard code generation benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP), as well as new adversarially-perturbed test sets. Baselines include standard few-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics will include pass@k accuracy, robustness to adversarial inputs, and qualitative analysis of generated code diversity and error types."
}
